title: VPC Subnets, NAT, and Peering
description:
## Private instances need outbound updates only

Application instances live in a **private subnet** (no public IPs) and must download OS package updates and call a public API over the internet. They must **not** be reachable from the internet inbound. The VPC also peers with a second VPC, and a team asks whether peering to VPC-B automatically gives them reachability to VPC-C that VPC-B peers with. Which configuration is correct?

options.text: Place a NAT gateway in a public subnet and point the private subnet's route table default route (0.0.0.0/0) at the NAT gateway; the NAT gateway uses the public subnet's Internet Gateway route to reach the internet, enabling outbound-initiated connections while denying unsolicited inbound. VPC peering is non-transitive, so peering A↔B does not grant A reachability to C even if B↔C are peered.

Attach an Internet Gateway directly to the private subnet's route table; an Internet Gateway permits outbound traffic while blocking all inbound by default, so no NAT is needed. VPC peering is transitive, so A automatically reaches C through B.
Give each private instance a public IP and rely on security groups to block inbound; the NAT gateway is only for IPv6. Peering routes propagate transitively across all connected VPCs.

Put the NAT gateway in the private subnet itself and route 0.0.0.0/0 to it; the Internet Gateway is unnecessary because the NAT gateway performs both inbound and outbound translation. Peering is transitive but only for traffic under 1500 MTU.

explanation: A **NAT gateway lives in a public subnet** (one whose route table sends 0.0.0.0/0 to an Internet Gateway and which has been allocated a public/Elastic IP). The **private subnet's** route table sends its default route to the NAT gateway; the NAT gateway then forwards out via the Internet Gateway. This allows **outbound-initiated** flows (package updates, API calls) and their return traffic, while unsolicited **inbound** connections cannot reach the private instances because they have no public address and nothing routes inbound to them — exactly (a). An Internet Gateway alone does not give a no-public-IP instance internet access and does not by itself "allow outbound, block inbound" for private instances (b is wrong); routing a private subnet straight at an IGW makes it a public subnet, which still needs public IPs to work. Giving private instances public IPs (c) defeats the "not reachable inbound" requirement, and NAT is not "only for IPv6." A NAT gateway cannot sit in its own private subnet routing to itself, and it does not replace the IGW (d). Crucially, **VPC peering is non-transitive**: a route to a peered VPC is not propagated to that VPC's other peers, so A↔B and B↔C never imply A↔C — refuting (b), (c), and (d)'s transitivity claims.

hints.title: Public vs private subnet definition
hints.body: A subnet is 'public' if its route table has a route to an Internet Gateway. The NAT gateway must sit in such a subnet; the private subnet routes its default through the NAT gateway.

hints.title: NAT gateway direction
hints.body: NAT gateways allow connections initiated from inside out (and their replies) but not connections initiated from the internet inward — ideal for patch/update downloads with no inbound exposure.

hints.title: Peering is not transitive
hints.body: If A peers with B and B peers with C, A still cannot reach C. You must create explicit peering (and routes) for every pair, or use a transit gateway.
